children.name: Polioptila
children.description: Gnatcatchers — tiny, active insectivorous birds of the Americas, constantly flicking their long, narrow tails upwards. Their blue-grey upperparts and slender bills give them the appearance of miniature mockingbirds. They are among the most energetic of small passerines, ceaselessly gleaning insects from leaves and twigs.

children.name: Polioptila caerulea
children.description: The only gnatcatcher breeding in the United States and Canada. Delicate blue-grey above, white below, with a long white-edged tail that is constantly cocked and flicked. Its call is a thin, nasal 'spee' reminiscent of a Northern Parula. Inhabits deciduous woodland and scrub. Migrates to the southern US, Mexico, and the Caribbean for winter.

children.name: Polioptila melanura
children.description: A resident gnatcatcher of arid scrublands in the southwestern United States and Mexico. Very similar to the Blue-grey Gnatcatcher but distinguished by its all-black tail (seen from below) and more extensive black on the crown in breeding males. Inhabits desert washes and thorn scrub.

children.name: Polioptila californica
children.description: A coastal California and Baja California endemic. Formerly considered a subspecies of the Black-tailed Gnatcatcher. Darker below than its relative. Its habitat — coastal sage scrub — has been heavily impacted by urban development, making it a Threatened species in California.

children.name: Polioptila attenboroughi
children.description: Named after Sir David Attenborough, this recently discovered gnatcatcher is endemic to the Inambari River basin in southeastern Peru. Described as a new species in 2023, it was split from the Tropical Gnatcatcher complex based on differences in vocalisations and plumage. Its discovery highlights the still-incomplete knowledge of Amazonian biodiversity.

children.name: Polioptila plumbea
children.description: A widespread lowland gnatcatcher found from Mexico through Central America to the Amazon Basin. Brownish-grey above, pale below, with a long white-tipped tail. The most common and widespread gnatcatcher in the Neotropics. Recent studies have split this complex into multiple species.

children.name: Polioptila nigriceps
children.description: A striking gnatcatcher with a glossy black cap extending to the nape. Found in western Mexico, occasionally straying into southern Arizona. Longer-billed than other North American gnatcatchers. Prefers arid thorn forest and tropical deciduous woodland.

children.name: Polioptila dumicola
children.description: A gnatcatcher of southern South America, found from Bolivia and Brazil south to Argentina and Uruguay. Blue-grey above, white below, with a distinctive black mask in breeding males. Inhabits gallery forest, woodland, and scrub. The southernmost gnatcatcher species.

children.name: Polioptila leucogastra
children.description: A gnatcatcher with white belly from Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Polioptila bilineata
children.description: A gnatcatcher from the Choco region of Colombia and Ecuador.

children.name: Polioptila facilis
children.description: A gnatcatcher from the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Polioptila guianensis
children.description: A gnatcatcher from the Guiana region.

children.name: Polioptila schistaceigula
children.description: A gnatcatcher from the western Amazon.

children.name: Polioptila lactea
children.description: A gnatcatcher from the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Polioptila clemensis
children.description: A gnatcatcher endemic to Cozumel Island, Mexico.

children.name: Polioptila albiloris
children.description: A gnatcatcher from Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Microbates
children.description: Gnatwrens — tiny, stub-tailed wren-like birds of Neotropical forests. Despite their name, they are not wrens but belong to the gnatcatcher family. They skulk in dense undergrowth, constantly flicking their wings and tail, and are more often heard than seen. Their songs are thin, high-pitched trills.

children.name: Microbates cinereiventris
children.description: Found from Costa Rica through the Amazon Basin to Bolivia. Olive-brown above, pale grey below, with a distinctive white throat and white crescent below the eye. Its thin, high-pitched song is a characteristic sound of lowland rainforest understory.

children.name: Microbates collaris
children.description: A striking gnatwren of the Amazon Basin, with a bold black collar across the upper breast. Rufous-brown above, white below. Skulks in dense riverside vegetation. Its range stretches from Colombia and Venezuela to the Guianas and Brazil.

children.name: Ramphocaenus
children.description: Long-billed gnatwrens — a monotypic genus of tiny Neotropical birds with an extraordinarily long, fine bill used for extracting insects from crevices. Unlike the closely related Microbates, they have a longer bill and a more upright posture. They inhabit tangled undergrowth and vine tangles in lowland forests.

children.name: Ramphocaenus melanurus
children.description: A tiny, long-billed insectivore found from southern Mexico through Central America to Bolivia and Brazil. Brown above, pale buff below, with a very long, slender bill and a long, graduated tail. Its song is a rapid series of descending notes. Often forages in mixed-species flocks in the forest understory.
